annotations.text: The most compelling and statistically most likely diagnosis is Richter transformation (RT), the transformation of CLL into a high-grade lymphoma, most commonly diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL).

the acute nature of her symptoms, the dramatic metabolic activity on PET-CT, the presence of central necrosis, and the development of systemic B symptoms over a 2-week period strongly suggest that this is not merely a progression of indolent CLL.
If granulomas were present, the diagnosis would be far more compelling, but their absence makes infection less likely as the primary driver.

The clinical hallmarks of RT include rapid lymphadenopathy, B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and new-onset cytopenias, all of which are present here.
We must carefully weigh the evidence for and against each major diagnostic category, considering the patient’s immunocompromised state, prior treatments, and the imaging and histopathologic findings.
The necrosis and intense metabolic activity strongly argue that a large-cell component is present, even if not yet morphologically apparent.

the constellation of findings—rapidly enlarging, necrotic, intensely FDG-avid inguinal lymphadenopathy in a patient with long-standing CLL, new-onset systemic B symptoms, and a biopsy showing a monomorphic CLL-like infiltrate—is most consistent with Richter transformation, likely to DLBCL, with possible under-sampling of the large-cell component.
RT occurs in approximately 2–10% of CLL patients over their lifetime, with a median time to transformation of 5–7 years from diagnosis—though it can occur decades later, as in this case.
The patient’s history of prior surgery for a pelvic mass initially thought to be ovarian but later confirmed as CLL suggests that her disease may have been present in extranodal sites for decades.
